text: In this video, robot-assisted laparoscopic adrenalectomy will be demonstrated for a four centimeter pheochromocytoma. The objectives discussed in this video will be the case of a forty-four year old male with intermittent hypertension refractory to multiple antihypertensive agents, port placement, and some technical aspects of left robotic adrenalectomy. This patient underwent extensive endocrinologic workup and CT imaging revealed a four centimeter adrenal mass. Pheochromocytoma was at the top of the differential diagnosis.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position for the robotic left adrenalectomy. A 12mm camera trocar is inserted in the left midclavicular line, approximately 10cm above the umbilicus. An 8mm left arm trocar is placed 7cm superior to the camera trocar, while the right arm trocar is positioned 7cm inferior and slightly lateral to the camera trocar. The fourth robotic arm is inserted 5cm inferior and 4cm lateral to the right arm. A 12mm assistant port is placed in the midline above the umbilicus.

The surgeon begins the procedure by reflecting the descending colon medially. As the dissection progresses towards the spleen, the peritoneum lateral to the spleen is incised cephalad, allowing for superior medial reflection of the spleen. The robotic fourth arm is used for retraction, applying superior medial pressure on the spleen to develop the plane between the spleen and the superior medial aspect of the kidney. The fourth arm also applies anterior pressure on the lower pole of the left kidney, facilitating the identification of the left gonadal vein. The dissection then continues cephalad towards the left renal vein.

The surgeon divides the tissue medial to the left renal vein to enhance its exposure. Following the dissection, the left adrenal vein is identified on the cephalad aspect of the left renal vein, ligated with two hemlock clips, and then divided. Subsequently, the tissue inferior and medial to the adrenal gland is divided, and the fourth robotic arm is used to retract the adrenal gland, aiding in its final extirpation.
The surgeon places the excised adrenal gland specimen into a retrieval bag for removal from the surgical field. The specimen is confirmed to be a margin-negative four centimeter pheochromocytoma.
